speaker: interviewer
text: Today is April 9, 1995. This is an interview with Max Durst. I am Amy Friedman. We are in Little Neck, Long Island in New York in the United States of America. And we'll be speaking in English. OK. It's April 9, 1995. And this is an interview with Max Durst. I'm Amy Friedman. And we're in Little Neck, Long Island in New York in the United States of America. And we're speaking in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Why don't we start by telling us your name and where you were born and when.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Max Durst. I was born on January the 9th, 1927 in Cieszyn, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about Cieszyn-- life in Cieszyn, Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Cieszyn was a part-- is a city in a region of Poland which was known as Silesia. Our town was basically the border between Poland and Czechoslovakia. Our part of the town had a population of approximately 20,000. We had a river that ran between the Polish and Czech sides. The name of the river was the Olza. There was a bridge whereby-- which separated the two countries. And of course, we could-- being residents of the Polish side of Cieszyn, we could, by special pass, cross over to the Czech side of Cieszyn. Most of my family, my father's family, lived on the Czech side of Cieszyn. Therefore, we practically were every day, my family and myself, in the Czech side. But of course, we lived on the Polish side.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between the people of the one side versus the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you-- are you referring to family people or just people in general?

speaker: interviewer
text: Both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of the family people that lived in Poland, of course, they sort of chosen to live in Poland. And that was because they lived in the Poland-- in the Polish side before World War I. And they remained in that part. And of course, Poland and Czechoslovakia were created shortly after World War I. And as far as the population, I don't think that the Polish and Czech populations were particularly fond of each other. They were sort of-- they're both Slavic countries, but not particularly friendly to each other. Each side had a gripe about the other. We as Jews didn't particularly take part in it because we lived very well. I mean, we got along very well with the Jewish population of from the Czech side. As a matter of fact, our temple-- we belonged to the temple which was in the Czech side. And it was really very easy for us to cross over. All we need is a yearly passport for which we paid a relatively small fee, and we could just go as many times, communicate. We could walk over the bridge. The town, being a small town, was relatively easy. So from the place where we lived-- from the apartment house that we lived to the apartment house where my relatives lived on the Czech side was approximately maybe a 15 to 20 minutes walk. So we took advantage of it, of course. And we-- the family was close family, and we saw each other every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us more about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was an only son. Basically, I was an only son because I had a older-- I had an older brother. My older brother died shortly after I was born. I believe I was a year old when my brother died. It was during an diphtheria epidemic at the time. Now, I was born in 1927, and my understanding is that he has died in 1928. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I attended public school in my hometown of Cieszyn. And I was also a member of a Zionist organization, of a Zionist youth organization. It was known as the Betar at the time. It was very, very social. And of course, we were-- we were learning the virtues of the Jewish homeland, of the future Jewish homeland. Furthermore, it was also well organized because we had-- we had outings. We played a lot of ping pong. And it gave us something to do, so to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the ideology of Betar?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the revisionistic organization of the Zionist movement. I believe that is now the Likud. It's known as the Likud in Israel. In those days, it was called the Betar. Today it is called the Likud. I don't know if it is as-- if it is exactly the same, but it's the follow up of the-- the Likud is really the follow up of the Betar. The founder of this organization was Vladimir Jabotinsky. He was the leader at the time of the entire movement. And as a matter of fact, Menachem Begin, which later became Prime Minister of Israel, he was the head of the Polish faction of the Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a kid in Betar, what were your feelings? What were your thoughts about the world and yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were not particularly interested-- being that I was at the time, when I joined I might have been anywhere-- I think I was 9 or 10 years old. Politics did not play a very important part of my life in that time. I was also busy with school. And I was busy going to getting a Hebrew education. I did go to Hebrew school, where I learned some Hebrew. I was also taking English lessons. One of my aunts, my father's sister, was an English teacher. And she lived in our town for a while. And I was very fortunate because she was giving me some English lessons on and off, whereby I got my basic English training at the time. Which at the time was very valuable, because being a young child, it sticks better. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to your family. Can you tell us about your mom and dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father was a fruit merchant, wholesale fruit merchant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were not particularly wealthy, but we were comfortable. We always had a maid, which at that particular time in Poland was not a very difficult thing because it was rather easily obtainable. It was not excessively expensive. And it was a help because she took care of me when my father was busy in business. My mother sometimes helped along in business. So I had somebody that could take care of me at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel Mendel. My mother's name was Dora. My mother had a sister that lived in Krakow. My father had two brothers. And he also had three sisters. My father was the oldest of them all. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I also had a grandfather who lived in-- it was my father's father, paternal grandfather. And he lived with his second wife in the Czech side of Cieszyn. And of course, we visited him very quite frequently as well. He was also very active in the synagogue. And he was actually the gabbai in the synagogue. That is-- a gabbai in the synagogue is sort of a-- what should I say-- he's sort of an honorary title of a caretaker. He distributes the [NON-ENGLISH] and so on and so forth, which was strictly voluntary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was tall, always had a grey beard, very religious, and so was my father. My father was also religious. Whenever he did not go to temple, he always did his tefillin in the morning. He never ate before he did his morning prayer. And we were quite observant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about Jewish life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish life? Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: For you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in addition to going to Hebrew school, I was also going to Cheder, which was at that time more of-- getting more of a Talmudical education. Unfortunately, not very much stuck to it, [LAUGHS] but nonetheless, I was eight years a student in Cheder. So I was kept quite busy. Between my Zionist organization, my normal school, my occasional English lesson, my Hebrew school, it didn't leave me too much time to play. But I-- I managed. I managed to play soccer a little, play some other games with my-- with my peers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends? Were they Jews, non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Primarily Jews. We were not peculiarly comfortable associating with our Polish Gentile friends. We did not care to associate with them because they actually didn't want to associate with us either. They called us all kinds of names. It was not a very congenial atmosphere. We felt the anti-Semitic environment which was surrounding us. And furthermore, even in school the teachers, which were primarily Polish Gentile teachers, and to a great extent they made anti-Semitic remarks directed towards the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the usual. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nothing very flattering towards the Jews-- dirty Jews, so on and so forth. And that, mind you, also came from a teacher. And you could see a certain type of favoritism, which was extended towards the non-Jewish children as opposed to the Jewish children. Whenever there was a fight which ensued between the Gentile boy and the Jewish boy, it was always the Jewish boy that was at fault, obviously. So we-- but we particularly didn't pay too much attention to it because as soon as school was over we went our way, and, of course, they went their way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any stories of anti-Semitism that you remember your own-- your own experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- in our town, there was a-- there were incidences where young Jewish men-- by young Jewish men, I mean Jewish men in their late teens-- were attacked by students, by Gentile students. So they have organized sort of a self-defense. And we-- they have recruited-- of course, I was not part of it. I was only-- at the time when the war broke out, I was only 12 years old. But they have organized sort of a self-defense team. And whenever they have to go somewhere, they tried to go in a group-- safety in numbers. [LAUGHS] Now, I particular remember one incident where they went. They were sitting in a restaurant, in a Jewish-owned restaurant, a coffee shop so to say, and they were having their coffee or whatever. And a group of Polish students, college students, came in and started making trouble. And the owner, who was Jewish, asked them politely to leave. And they, of course, started a fight. And the Jewish group stood up and being-- they were actually attacked. And they actually gave them a-- a-- a little beating there that they'll never forget. It just so happened that there was one particular young man who was quite muscular, quite strong. I guess you would call him-- in this country, you would call him a typical football player. [LAUGHS] And he gave them a licking that they-- I don't think that they would ever come-- came back to that particular place. And it was quickly forgotten, of course. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that for you to hear that story, when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you heard that story, was that a rumor that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was sort of a satisfaction. After, after hearing stories whereby we were on the receiving end, it turned out that somebody else was on the receiving end this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that the Jews didn't get along with the Poles. The Jews and the Jews got along across the bridge. How was that received by the Poles and the Czechs?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think they particularly-- to, to them, at least to the Polish population on the Polish side, the Polish Gentile population, the fact that we got along with the Jews-- it was just a natural because we were all Jews, so all the same type. So it really didn't matter to them. We never really had any disagreements because we were all, of course, part of the same group, socioeconomic group, and so on and so forth. But by and large, as I mentioned before, the Polish Gentiles did not get along with the Czech Gentiles. As a matter of fact, the Poles claimed that the Czech part of Cieszyn was really a Polish-- was part of Poland. It should have never been given to the Czechs. And therefore, if you remember history, in 1938 when the Nazis marched into Czechoslovakia, which was-- oh, it was before they actually went, when Czechoslovakia was divided, which was the famous Chamberlain agreement. Poland, the Polish government, took possession of the Czech part of Cieszyn. It was not only the Czech part of Cieszyn, but it was also the surrounding area. So for a while-- for approximately a little over a year, there was no more Czech. It was all Polish. It all belonged to Poland then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your experience of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was annexed. It was annexed by Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your experience of that, your own story? How did that affect your, your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not at all. It didn't affect us in any way because we were free to go before to the Czech side. The only time-- the only difference was that we no longer needed the passport to cross over to the other side. Other than that, it really didn't affect us in any way. We sort of felt sorry for the Czech population because we always found the Czech people-- and I'm referring to Gentile Czech people-- they were much more tolerant of their Jewish population than was the Polish Gentile population. They were more understanding. They were-- they were easier to get along with. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Polish annexed the Czech side, was that at all threatening?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- well, that by itself was not threatening. But what we-- my understanding-- and my understanding was primarily from what I heard from grown-ups-- was that the Poles, the Polish government, has contributed to the downfall of Czechoslovakia because the Germans grabbed the Sudetenland. The Hungarians grabbed part of Czechoslovakia which was known as Podkarpatska Rus. And the Poles grabbed the part that was known as Zaolzie, which sort of-- that's finished up the Czech Republic at the time. Which was sad because out of the Eastern European countries, I think their democracy was probably the best functioned democracy. They really were. And it was a pity to see a Czech Republic being swallowed up by three neighbors, so to say. And we just wondered how long it'll be before the Germans will swallow up Poland altogether. Which as history, as it turns out in retrospect, it didn't take very long. It took an extra year or so, and they swallowed up Poland, and then, later on the rest of Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you bring us through a Shabbat, a typical Shabbat. We want to hear your story, your own personal story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Typical Shabbat, yes. We always went to shul on Friday. At least, I went with my father to shul on Friday night. And of course, we walked because we didn't have a car. Very few people in our town had cars. And after services-- by the way, for Friday night, we went to our-- we did not go to the shul on the Czech side. But on our street there was a shul, which even though we belonged for the-- for any holiday we went to the Czech side, because that's where my grandfather was the official in there. He was the gabbai. We went to a temple, to a shul, on our street which we were members as well. And after that, after usually-- there was usually the traditional Shabbos dinner. And obviously, nobody went to work on Shabbat morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your Shabbat dinner like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Shabbat dinner was a traditional type, with fish, and soup, and challah, and boiled chicken, and, and boiled fruit, dessert, and so on and so forth. And whenever there was a stranger in town, we always made sure that that stranger was well taken care of. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Saturdays of course we went all-- well, all my father and myself-- we went to shul. And I must say that morning, usually, very little time was spent inside the shul. My friends and I, we were always outside the shul doing our thing-- running around, playing, doing the things that we weren't supposed to do, but we did them anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like what? Like playing, playing all kinds of games that boys do, catching each other and so on and so forth. And of course, after we went to lunch, we went back home. And in the afternoon, we went to our Zionist organization, which we had our games there, whatever we were supposed to do. We played ping pong. We had our meetings. We had our outings. And that was usually the day of fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at age 12, what were you expecting your life to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was expecting to study as much as possible. And then, we really did not expect to stay in Poland very much longer because the climate in Poland was such-- and I don't mean the weather climate. When I'm-- when I'm referring to climate, I mean the social climate was such where it was sort of difficult for a Jew to stay in Poland. You don't always want to be oppressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we eventually hoped to emigrate to either-- more than likely Pale-- which was at that time know as Palestine, hoping that we will have our own homeland. But for the time being, we lived in Poland. And we hoped to be able to eventually emigrate to Palestine. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as you well know, in 1939-- and I am now backtracking to some times-- I would say it must have been August 1939. The Nazis were making demands of Poland. Now that Czechoslovakia was swallowed up, Poland was next on the line. And it's gotten to a point where we expected that the war is really inevitable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my family and I felt we are a bit too close to the German border. Basically, our town was located no more than 30 minutes by car from the German border. So we felt, it's time to move on temporarily, hoping that it will blow over. So what we did is we packed few possessions. And we moved over. We moved to Krakow. As I mentioned before, my mother had a sister living in Krakow with her husband. So we temporarily moved in with them, and hoping that it will only be temporary because Hitler wouldn't dare to invade Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, to our not so much of a surprise, he did attack Poland on September 1, 1939. And within-- I think it was within two or three days the Germans marched into Krakow. By the way, our hometown was occupied the very first day. They were-- the war started somewhere around 5:00 AM. By 8:00 AM, the Germans already were in our hometown, as I later found out. It didn't really matter because the Germans came in three days later to Krakow anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I backtrack for one moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your other-- to your father's father and all the people on Czech side? Were they with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not with us. What they did is-- my relatives from the Czech side-- I'll start with my grandfather. My grandfather and his second wife had relatives in a town near Krakow known as Tarnow. So they ran off to Tarnow. My uncle-- his name is Jacob-- he and his wife and two sons [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] tried to get away from-- they got away from Cieszyn shortly before the war. I would say it must have been-- we left Cieszyn about a month before the war or thereabouts. He left about a week before the war. And they just kept going east. And my aunt, Jacob's wife, and their youngest son had relatives in the Polish town of Gorlice, so they stayed in Gorlice. My father's brother, Jacob, and his son, Josef they kept on going further east and ended up in Lemberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going stop the tape just for a second.
